title: Anglo-Indian house curry powder
subtitle: A toasted and ground spice blend for curries
excerpt: Coriander, cumin, turmeric, chilli, fenugreek, and black pepper toasted and ground to a warm, all-purpose curry powder. The Anglo-Indian kitchen blend that preceded ready-made powder, balanced for general use in meat curries, soups, and devilled preparations.
sourceNotes: Based on curry powder formulas documented in Colonel A.R. Kenney-Herbert (Wyvern), 'Culinary Jottings for Madras' (1878, public domain) and Lizzie Collingham, 'Curry: A Tale of Cooks and Conquerors' (2005). The Anglo-Indian house curry powder was a practical blend for general use, not a specific regional masala, each household had its own proportions.

content.text: A house curry powder was a kitchen staple in Anglo-Indian homes. One blend used across many dishes rather than a different mix for each. It was always made at home, from whole spices toasted and ground fresh. The exact recipe varied by household and region.

This blend is built around a high ratio of coriander and cumin, tempered with fenugreek for bitterness, turmeric for colour, and black pepper for heat rather than chilli. The chilli level is mild. It can always be increased in individual dishes. Toast the whole spices dry before grinding; the flavour difference is significant.

Combine {{coriander-seeds}} of coriander seeds, {{cumin-seeds}} of cumin seeds, {{black-pepper}} whole peppercorns, {{fenugreek-seeds}} of fenugreek seeds, {{mustard-seeds}} of mustard seeds, the seeds from {{cardamom-pods}} cardamom pods, {{cloves}} cloves, and the broken {{cinnamon-stick}} in a dry pan over a medium heat. Toast, shaking constantly, for 3 to 4 minutes until the spices smell fragrant and begin to darken slightly. Do not let them smoke. Tip onto a cold plate and leave to cool for 5 minutes.

Grind the cooled toasted spices to a fine powder in a spice grinder. Add {{turmeric-ground}} of ground turmeric, {{chilli-flakes}} of chilli, and {{ginger-ground}} of dried ground ginger. Stir to combine. Store in a sealed jar, away from light. Use within 3 months for best flavour.

items.fix: Always toast whole spices before grinding. Discard any batch more than 3 months old.
items.cause: Spices not toasted, or kept too long

items.fix: Fenugreek goes bitter quickly. Toast it briefly, just until it starts to colour. Reduce the quantity to half a teaspoon if the bitterness is too strong in the finished blend.
items.symptom: Fenugreek makes the blend too bitter

content.text: House curry powder was as basic to an Anglo-Indian kitchen as salt and pepper. The Victorian cookery writer Colonel Kenney-Herbert pressed cooks to make their own blend rather than buy shop powder, which he found stale and weak. His mix, heavy on coriander and balanced with cumin, fenugreek and black pepper, is the model for this version. In the twentieth century shop-bought curry powder took over and home-grinding faded. The fresh blend is still much more fragrant.
